key_insight: Explainable machine-learning models for GVHD prediction showed modest internal performance but only weak-to-moderate external discrimination, highlighting the challenge of transporting transplant risk models across centers.
clinical_relevance: For allo-HSCT teams, the study suggests that predicted GVHD probabilities should be calibrated and revalidated locally before being used to support clinical decisions.
method: Developed CatBoost models on 3 registry-derived datasets (N=2,509) for acute and chronic GVHD prediction.
Externally validated across 6 independent cohorts (N=14,788), including adult, pediatric, and Middle Eastern populations.
Assessed discrimination, calibration (intercept/slope), and interpretability with SHAP; explored overall survival with a Cox model using predicted acute-GVHD risk.
evidence_signal: Internal AUROC was 0.63 for acute GVHD and 0.72 for chronic GVHD, while external AUROC ranged from 0.51-0.57 and 0.54-0.64; survival model external C-index was 0.47-0.53.
watch_item: Calibration drift and heterogeneous feature availability across cohorts may limit real-world deployment unless models are locally recalibrated and prospectively tested.
paper_title: External Validation and Calibration Assessment of Explainable Machine Learning Models for GVHD Prediction After Allogeneic HSCT
